text: Chapter 2 drills the three stroke types named in Chapter 1 as standalone exercises applied to all strings. The Down-up stroke is positioned as the default guitar stroke and is used to train downbeat accents, while the Up-down stroke inverts that accent so the upstroke carries the downbeat, and Mixed strokes synthesize the two by alternating accents between down and up. Throughout, Bruno restates the load-bearing right-hand mechanics: wrist straight but not stiff with motion coming from the elbow (Wrist and elbow mechanics), and a strict no-anchor posture - the right-hand fingers must not touch the guitar, strings, or pick guard (Right-hand finger contact rule, Pick guard contact rule). The chapter closes with the In-between stroke, identified as the hardest to master because it requires moving between adjacent strings; it is drilled on adjacent-string double-stops with explicit instruction not to let notes ring. Together these exercises introduce accent control, free-floating right-hand posture, and the adjacent-string double-stop as the canonical vehicle for the most difficult stroke.
